sections.heading: Classification and Definition
sections.content: Penile cancer is a rare malignancy arising from the penis, with squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) accounting for approximately 95% of cases.

sections.heading: Anatomy and Embryology
sections.content: The penis is an external male genital organ composed of the glans, prepuce (foreskin), and shaft. Embryologically, the penis develops from the genital tubercle. The glans and prepuce are derived from ectodermal tissue, while the corpora cavernosa and corpus spongiosum are of mesodermal origin.

sections.heading: Histology
sections.content: The mucosal surface of the glans and inner prepuce is lined by non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium, which is the site of origin for most penile cancers.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.content: The disease can present as a visible or palpable lesion on the penis, which may be associated with pain, discharge, bleeding, or foul odor. The lesion may be characterized as nodular, ulcerative, or fungating. Advanced disease may involve palpable inguinal lymph nodes and/or systemic symptoms.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Importance
sections.content: Early diagnosis is crucial as penile cancer can lead to disfigurement and significant morbidity. [1,2]
